# Preface {.unnumbered}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

```{r}
#| label: preface-setup
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| comment: NA

easypackages::packages(
  "bslib",
  "cols4all", "covr", "cowplot",
  "dendextend", "digest","DiagrammeR","dtwclust", "downlit",
  "e1071", "exactextractr","elevatr",
  "FNN", "future", "forestdata",
  "gdalcubes", "gdalUtilities", "geojsonsf", "geos", "ggplot2", "ggstats",
  "ggspatial", "ggmap", "ggplotify", "ggpubr", "ggrepel", "giscoR",
  "hdf5r", "httr", "httr2", "htmltools",
  "jsonlite",
  "kohonen",
  "leaflet.providers", "leafem", "libgeos","luz","lwgeom", "leaflet", "leafgl",
  "mapedit", "mapview", "maptiles", "methods", "mgcv",
  "ncdf4", "nnet",
  "openxlsx", "parallel", "plotly",
  "randomForest", "rasterVis", "raster", "Rcpp", "RcppArmadillo",
  "RcppCensSpatial","rayshader", "RcppEigen", "RcppParallel",
  "RColorBrewer", "reactable", "rgl", "rsconnect","RStoolbox", "rts",
  "s2", "sf", "scales", "sits","spdep", "stars", "stringr","supercells",
  "terra", "testthat", "tidyverse", "tidyterra","tools",
  "tmap", "tmaptools", "terrainr",
  "xgboost",
  prompt = F)

sf::sf_use_s2(use_s2 = F)

knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = here::here())

options(repos = c(CRAN = "https://cloud.r-project.org"),
        htmltools.dir.version = FALSE,
        htmltools.preserve.raw = FALSE)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE,
  error = FALSE, comment = NA,
  tidy.opts = list(width.cutoff = 60))

# Previously this chunk initialised rgee / reticulate against Google Earth
# Engine and pulled AOI + land-cover as live ee objects, then every map below
# drew its layers from ee_tile_url(). Tile URLs carry a session token that
# expires within weeks, so rendered pages went blank for the reader.
#
# All GEE extraction now runs once in gee/export_to_asset.py and is mirrored
# to data/rendered/*.tif by gee/download_cogs.R. This chunk loads those
# committed COGs and vector boundaries, so rendering no longer depends on
# any live GEE session.
source(here::here("R", "palettes.R"))
source(here::here("R", "local_rasters.R"))

aoi_country_sf <- local_aoi("country")
aoi_states_sf  <- local_aoi("states")

lc_2020        <- local_raster("mcd12q1_igbp_2020")
all_years_burn <- local_raster("mcd64a1_burn_2000_2025_max")
burn_edges     <- local_burn_edges()
```

```{css, echo=FALSE, class.source = 'foldable'}
div.column {
    display: inline-block;
    vertical-align: top;
    width: 50%;
}

#TOC::before {
  content: "";
  display: block;
  height:200px;
  width: 200px;
  background-image: url('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/seamusrobertmurphy/IPCC-wildfire-emissions/refs/heads/main/01-burn-area/AOI/aoi_burn_aa.png');
  background-size: contain;
  background-position: 50% 50%;
  padding-top: 80px !important;
  background-repeat: no-repeat;
}
```

This eBook demonstrates the IPCC Tier 1 method for estimating greenhouse gas emissions from biomass burning, implementing Equation 2.27 from the 2019 IPCC Refinement [@ipccChapter2Generic2019] end-to-end: satellite-based burned area detection, land cover stratification into fire categories, assignment of default fuel consumption values, and application of emission factors to derive CH~4~, N~2~O, and CO~2~ estimates formatted for UNFCCC reporting. All workflows use Honduras as the example AOI. Paged links to source materials are provided in footnotes throughout.

Under the Managed Land Proxy (MLP), all fires on managed land — including natural wildfires — count toward a country's reported LULUCF balance. The 2019 Refinement (Section 2.6) provides optional guidance for disaggregating natural disturbance spikes from background anthropogenic fire activity. This eBook implements both the standard Tier 1 calculation and the disaggregation framework, structured in four chapters:

1.  [Burned Area](https://seamusrobertmurphy.quarto.pub/ipcc-fire-emissions/01-burn-area/): Extract and validate burned area from MODIS MCD64A1, apply uncertainty filters, aggregate temporally, and compare against alternative fire products.
2.  [Fuel Stratification](https://seamusrobertmurphy.quarto.pub/ipcc-fire-emissions/02-fuel-stratification/): Classify burned pixels into fire categories using land cover, climate zones, and soil classification.
3.  [Fuel Consumption](https://seamusrobertmurphy.quarto.pub/ipcc-fire-emissions/03-fuel-consumption/): Assign IPCC default fuel consumption factors (M~B~ × C~f~) by vegetation type and climate zone.
4.  [Emission Factors](https://seamusrobertmurphy.quarto.pub/ipcc-fire-emissions/04-emission-factors/): Apply emission factors (G~ef~) via Equation 2.27, compute CH₄ and N₂O emissions, and format for UNFCCC CRF reporting.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Tier 1 Stratification Datasets[^index-1] {.unnumbered}

-   [MODIS MCD64A1 C6.1](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/MODIS_061_MCD64A1): Monthly burned area (500 m, 2000–present). GEE: `MODIS/061/MCD64A1`. Bands: `BurnDate`, `Uncertainty`, `QA`.
-   [MODIS MCD12Q1 C6.1](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/MODIS_061_MCD12Q1): IGBP annual land cover (500 m). GEE: `MODIS/061/MCD12Q1`. Band: `LC_Type1`.
-   [MODIS MOD14A1 C6.1](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/MODIS_061_MOD14A1): Daily thermal anomalies / active fires (1 km). GEE: `MODIS/061/MOD14A1`. Band: `FireMask`.
-   [IPCC Global Climate Regions](https://philipaudebert.users.earthengine.app/view/ipcc-climate-zones): 12-zone classification derived per IPCC 2006 [& 2019](https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2019rf/pdf/4_Volume4/19R_V4_Ch03_Land%20Representation.pdf#page=47) guidelines, maintained by [ESDAC](https://esdac.jrc.ec.europa.eu/content/support-renewable-energy-directive#tabs-0-description=1) and individual [contributors](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.7303807).
-   [IPCC Global Soil Zones](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.catena.2024.108258): 13-class soil classification from HWSD v2.0 via WRB-to-IPCC taxotransfer [@batjes2009ipcc; @batjes2010ipcc] ([GEE Asset](https://code.earthengine.google.com/?asset=projects/murphys-deforisk/assets/ipcc_soils)).
-   [FAO Agro-Ecological Zones](https://data.apps.fao.org/catalog/iso/2fb209d0-fd34-4e5e-a3d8-a13c241eb61b): Climate-vegetation framework for fuel consumption stratification ([V4](https://data.apps.fao.org/catalog/dataset/global-agro-ecological-zones-global-10km-gaez-v4), [V5](https://data.apps.fao.org/gaez/)).
-   [FAO GAUL 2015](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/FAO_GAUL_2015_level1): Administrative boundaries. GEE: `FAO/GAUL/2015/level0`, `FAO/GAUL/2015/level1`.

## Validation Datasets {.unnumbered}

-   [Landsat 8 C2 T1 L2](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/LANDSAT_LC08_C02_T1_L2): Surface reflectance (30 m). Used for dNBR validation.
-   [Hansen Global Forest Change v1.12](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/UMD_hansen_global_forest_change_2024_v1_12): Tree canopy cover 2000 (30 m).
-   [ESA WorldCover 2020 v100](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/ESA_WorldCover_v100_2020): Global land cover (10 m).
-   [WorldClim V1 Bioclimatic Variables](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/WORLDCLIM_V1_BIO): 1 km climatologies (`bio01`, `bio12`) for climate zone delineation.
-   [USGS SRTM DEM](https://developers.google.com/earth-engine/datasets/catalog/USGS_SRTMGL1_003): 30 m elevation for lowland organic soil proxy.
-   [FAOSTAT Emissions from Fires](https://www.fao.org/faostat/en/#data/GI): Country-level fire emissions (1990–2024) using IPCC Tier 1 defaults. IPCC-recommended for NGHGI QA/QC (FAO, [2025](https://files-faostat.fao.org/production/GI/GI_e.pdf)).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## IPCC Tier 1 Fire Emissions

Equation 2.27[^index-2] [@ipccChapter2Generic2019]:

$$
L_{fire} = A \times M_B \times C_f \times G_{ef} \times 10^{-3}
$$

Where:

-   L~fire~ = greenhouse gas emissions from fire (tonnes of CH₄, N₂O, or CO₂)
-   A = area burnt (ha)
-   M~B~ = mass of fuel available for combustion (tonnes ha⁻¹)
-   C~f~ = combustion factor (dimensionless, proportion of fuel consumed)
-   G~ef~ = emission factor (g kg⁻¹ dry matter burnt)

Each term is stratified by fire category. FAOSTAT reclassifies MODIS MCD12Q1 IGBP 17-class land cover into three categories, each with distinct gas reporting rules:

Fire Category

|   | IGBP Source | Non-CO₂ via Eq. 2.27 | CO₂ Treatment |
|------------------|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| Forest remaining Forest (classes 1–5) | Evergreen/Deciduous Needleleaf & Broadleaf, Mixed | CH₄, N₂O | CO₂ reported via stock-change (Eq. 2.7–2.14) — not synchronous with regrowth[^index-3] |
| Forest converted to other land | Same classes, land-use change detected | CH₄, N₂O, CO₂ | All gases via Eq. 2.27; reported under new land-use category |
| Savanna/Grassland (classes 6–10) | Shrublands, Woody Savannas, Savannas, Grasslands | CH₄, N₂O | CO₂ not reported — assumed synchronous with annual regrowth |
| Organic Soil (HWSD overlay) | Histosols, independent of land cover | CO₂, CH₄ | Direct reporting (Wetlands Supplement) |

For forest remaining forest, fire CO₂ is captured through live biomass stock-change accounting (Eq. 2.7–2.14) because emissions and regrowth are not synchronous (§2.4). At Tier 1, DOM CO₂ is assumed zero. For deforestation fires, all gases including CO₂ are reported via Equation 2.27, with care to avoid double-counting with Equation 2.23 (decay). Organic soil fires are validated for Southeast Asia only; FAOSTAT sets emissions to zero beyond Indonesia, Malaysia, and Brunei [@ipcc2013Supplement20062014]. Default parameters from Tables 2.4[^index-4] and 2.5[^index-5] are applied in Chapters 3 and 4.

Organic soil fires are currently validated for Southeast Asia only; FAOSTAT sets emissions to zero beyond Indonesia, Malaysia, and Brunei [@ipcc2013Supplement20062014]. Default fuel consumption (Table 2.4[^index-6]) and emission factors (Table 2.5[^index-7]) are applied in Chapters 3 and 4 respectively.

#### Managed Land Proxy

Under the MLP, all emissions on managed lands, including natural wildfires, are attributed as anthropogenic. A severe wildfire year can inflate reported emissions without reflecting any change in land management.

The 2019 Refinement (Section 2.6, [pp 2.67](https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2019rf/pdf/4_Volume4/19R_V4_Ch02_Generic%20Methods.pdf#page=67)) introduced an optional disaggregation approach to separate natural disturbance spikes from anthropogenic fluxes using a statistical threshold (commonly the 95th percentile). Three methodological debates remain active:

1.  Threshold definition: 95th percentile is recommended but not mandated; alternatives include 90th percentile or rolling mean + 2 SD
2.  Temporal window: IPCC implies 30+ years, but MODIS covers only 2001–present; Landsat extension introduces cross-sensor uncertainty
3.  CO₂ reporting pathway: forest-remaining-forest reports CO₂ via stock-change (not Eq. 2.27); deforestation fires report all gases via Eq. 2.27 under the new land-use category

Countries experiencing high inter-annual variability may disaggregate these fluxes while preserving MLP totals for official reporting [@ipccChapter2Generic2019, pp. 251].[^index-8]

[![](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/seamusrobertmurphy/IPCC-wildfire-emissions/refs/heads/main/resources/ipcc-tier-selection-fire-emissions.png){fig-align="center" width="50%"}](https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2019rf/pdf/4_Volume4/19R_V4_Ch02_Generic%20Methods.pdf#page=51)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Stratification Layers

The maps below visualize each stratification layer over the Honduras AOI used throughout this eBook.

### Burned Area by Climate Zones

MODIS MCD64A1 C6.1 provides monthly burned area at 500 m from 2001–present. FAOSTAT retains only pixels with uncertainty \< 20% and aggregates to annual country totals [@rossi2016faostat]. The IPCC Climate Zones layer (12 zones, 1985–2015 climatology with corrigenda) stratifies fuel consumption for savanna and organic soil fires.

```{r}
#| label: preface-burned-area-climate
#| code-summary: "MCD64A1 burned area composite with IPCC Climate Zones"
#| error: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false


tmap::tmap_mode("view")
tmap::tm_shape(aoi_states_sf) + tmap::tm_borders(col = "MAP_COLORS", lwd = 1) +
  tmap::tm_shape(aoi_country_sf) + tmap::tm_borders(col = "white", lwd = 1.5) +
  tm_local_categorical("ipcc_climate_zones_honduras", ipcc_climate_palette,
                       title = "IPCC Climate Zones") +
  tm_local_mask(all_years_burn, "red",
                title = "Burn Area",
                group = "Burn Areas (2000–2025)") +
  tm_local_mask(burn_edges, "#FFFF00",
                title = "Burn Edges",
                group = "Burn Areas (2000–2025)") +
  tmap::tm_add_legend(polygons = "fill",
    fill   = ipcc_climate_palette$colors,
    labels = ipcc_climate_palette$labels,
    title  = "IPCC Climate Zones") +
  tmap::tm_add_legend(polygons = "fill", fill = "red",
    labels = "Burn Area", title = "MCD64A1 (2000–2025)") +
  tmap::tm_scalebar(position = c("RIGHT", "BOTTOM"), text.size = 0.5) +
  tmap::tm_basemap("Esri.WorldImagery")
```

### Fire Types by Landcover

The IGBP 17-class land cover is reclassified into the three fire categories defined above. Toggle between the raw classification and the reclassified result.

```{r}
#| label: preface-igbp-map
#| code-summary: "IGBP 17-class land cover and IPCC fire categories (2020)"
#| error: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false


tmap::tmap_mode("view")
tmap::tm_shape(aoi_country_sf) + tmap::tm_borders(col = "white", lwd = 1.5) +
  tm_local_categorical("mcd12q1_igbp_2020", igbp_palette,
                       title = "IGBP 17-Class", group = "IGBP 17-Class") +
  (tmap::tm_shape(local_fire_categories()) +
     tmap::tm_raster(
       col.scale  = tmap::tm_scale_categorical(
         values = fire_categories_palette$colors,
         labels = fire_categories_palette$labels,
         value.na = NA),
       col.legend = tmap::tm_legend(title = "IPCC Fire Categories (2020)"),
       col_alpha  = 0.7,
       group      = "IPCC Fire Categories")) +
  tm_local_mask(all_years_burn, "red",
                title = "Burn Area",
                group = "Burn Areas (2000–2025)") +
  tm_local_mask(burn_edges, "#FFFF00",
                title = "Burn Edges",
                group = "Burn Areas (2000–2025)") +
  tmap::tm_add_legend(polygons = "fill", fill = "red",
    labels = "Burn Area", title = "MCD64A1 (2000–2025)") +
  tmap::tm_add_legend(polygons = "fill",
    fill   = fire_categories_palette$colors,
    labels = fire_categories_palette$labels,
    title  = "IPCC Fire Categories (2020)") +
  tmap::tm_scalebar(position = c("RIGHT", "BOTTOM"), text.size = 0.5) +
  tmap::tm_basemap("Esri.WorldImagery")
```

### Forest Type by Climate Zones

Tables 2.4 and 2.6 stratify fuel consumption (M~B~) and combustion factors (C~f~) by forest biome, primary tropical (moist/dry/open), secondary tropical (by age), boreal, eucalypt, and other temperate. Table 2.5 collapses emission factors to two rows: Tropical and Extra-tropical. FAOSTAT implements this as a four-way climate split on forest pixels (IGBP 1–5):

| Stratum        | Climate Criteria        | M~B~ (t DM ha⁻¹) | G~ef~ row      |
|----------------|-------------------------|------------------|----------------|
| Tropical Moist | T \> 18°C, P \> 1500 mm | 119.6–163.6      | Tropical       |
| Tropical Dry   | T \> 18°C, P ≤ 1500 mm  | 83.9             | Tropical       |
| Temperate      | 0°C \< T ≤ 18°C         | 50.4             | Extra-tropical |
| Boreal         | T ≤ 0°C                 | 41.0             | Extra-tropical |

: Forest strata linking climate thresholds to Tables 2.4 and 2.5 {.striped .hover}

```{r}
#| label: preface-forest-strata-map
#| code-summary: "Forest stratification: Tropical Moist / Dry / Temperate / Boreal"
#| error: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false


tmap::tmap_mode("view")
tmap::tm_shape(aoi_country_sf) + tmap::tm_borders(col = "white", lwd = 1.5) +
  (tmap::tm_shape(local_forest_strata()) +
     tmap::tm_raster(
       col.scale  = tmap::tm_scale_categorical(
         values = forest_strata_palette$colors,
         labels = forest_strata_palette$labels,
         value.na = NA),
       col.legend = tmap::tm_legend(title = "Forest Strata (Tables 2.4/2.5)"),
       col_alpha  = 0.7,
       group      = "Forest Climate Strata")) +
  tm_local_mask(all_years_burn, "red",
                title = "Burn Area",
                group = "Burn Areas (2000-2025)") +
  tm_local_mask(burn_edges, "#FFFF00",
                title = "Burn Edges",
                group = "Burn Areas (2000-2025)") +
  tmap::tm_add_legend(type = "fill",
    col    = forest_strata_palette$colors,
    labels = forest_strata_palette$labels,
    title  = "Forest Strata (Tables 2.4/2.5)") +
  tmap::tm_add_legend(polygons = "fill", fill = "red",
    labels = "Burn Area", title = "MCD64A1 (2020-2025)") +
  tmap::tm_scalebar(position = c("RIGHT", "BOTTOM"), text.size = 0.5) +
  tmap::tm_basemap("Esri.WorldImagery")
```

### IPCC Soil Zones

The IPCC soil classification maps global soils into 13 classes derived from the HWSD via WRB-to-IPCC taxotransfer procedures [@batjes2009ipcc; @batjes2010ipcc]. For fire emissions, the critical class is ORG (Histosols), which triggers peat fire emission factors from the Wetlands Supplement [@ipcc2013Supplement20062014; @ipcc2013RevisedSupplementary2014].

```{r}
#| label: preface-ipcc-soils-map
#| code-summary: "IPCC Soil Zones from HWSD v2.0 (WRB Fix)"
#| error: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
sf::sf_use_s2(use_s2 = F)


sf::sf_use_s2(use_s2 = F)

tmap::tmap_mode("view")
tmap::tm_shape(aoi_country_sf) + tmap::tm_borders(col = "white", lwd = 1.5) +
  tm_local_categorical("ipcc_soils_honduras", ipcc_soils_palette,
                       title = "IPCC Soil Groups") +
  tm_local_mask(all_years_burn, "red",
                title = "Burn Area",
                group = "Burn Areas (2000–2025)") +
  tm_local_mask(burn_edges, "#FFFF00",
                title = "Burn Edges",
                group = "Burn Areas (2020-2025)") +
  tmap::tm_add_legend(type = "fill",
    col    = ipcc_soils_palette$visible_colors,
    labels = ipcc_soils_palette$visible_labels,
    title  = "IPCC Soil Classes (HWSD v2.0)") +
  tmap::tm_add_legend(polygons = "fill", fill = "red",
    labels = "Burn Area", title = "MCD64A1 (2000–2025)") +
  tmap::tm_scalebar(position = c("RIGHT", "BOTTOM"), text.size = 0.5) +
  tmap::tm_basemap("Esri.WorldImagery")
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Uncertainty Overview

Each term in Equation 2.27 introduces uncertainty that compounds across the chain. At Tier 1, cumulative uncertainty is typically ±30% for burned area (MCD64A1 may underestimate by 20–30% due to small fires, canopy obscuration, and cloud contamination), ±30–50% for fuel consumption defaults (reflecting variability in vegetation, fire intensity, and moisture), and ±20–60% for emission factors (influenced by flaming vs. smoldering balance). Land classification accuracy (\~75% for MCD12Q1) directly affects which fuel values are assigned — confusion between woody savannas and forests spans a 2.6–164 t ha⁻¹ range in Table 2.4. Organic soil fires carry the highest uncertainty due to limited validation outside Southeast Asia. Uncertainty reduction strategies and inter-annual variability analysis are addressed in Chapter 4.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Google Earth Engine Setup

All spatial processing in this eBook uses [Google Earth Engine](https://earthengine.google.com/) (GEE) via the Python API, called from R through `reticulate`. GEE provides petabyte-scale imagery archives and planetary-scale computation, FAOSTAT uses the same platform for its pixel-level fire emission processing.

Getting started:

1.  Register at <https://earthengine.google.com/signup/>
2.  Install the Python API: `pip install earthengine-api`
3.  Authenticate: `earthengine authenticate`

R/reticulate setup (used throughout this eBook):

``` r
reticulate::use_python("/opt/local/bin/python", required = TRUE)
ee <- reticulate::import("ee")
ee$Initialize(project = "your-project-id")
```

Core GEE assets used:[^index-9]

``` javascript
// Burned Area
var burnedArea = ee.ImageCollection("MODIS/061/MCD64A1");
// Land Cover
var landCover  = ee.ImageCollection("MODIS/061/MCD12Q1");
// Country Boundaries
var countries  = ee.FeatureCollection("FAO/GAUL/2015/level0");
// IPCC Soil Zones
var ipccSoils  = ee.Image("projects/murphys-deforisk/assets/ipcc_soils");
// IPCC Climate Zones
var ipccClimate = ee.Image("users/philipaudebert/IPCC/Corrigenda/ipcc_climate_1985-2015_corrigenda_raster");
```

#### Local Environment Setup

```{r}
#| label: preface-setup-demo
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| comment: NA

easypackages::packages(
  "bslib",
  "cols4all", "covr", "cowplot",
  "dendextend", "digest","DiagrammeR","dtwclust", "downlit",
  "e1071", "exactextractr","elevatr",
  "FNN", "future", "forestdata",
  "gdalcubes", "gdalUtilities", "geojsonsf", "geos", "ggplot2", "ggstats",
  "ggspatial", "ggmap", "ggplotify", "ggpubr", "ggrepel", "giscoR",
  "hdf5r", "httr", "httr2", "htmltools",
  "jsonlite",
  "kohonen",
  "leaflet.providers", "leafem", "libgeos","luz","lwgeom", "leaflet", "leafgl",
  "mapedit", "mapview", "maptiles", "methods", "mgcv",
  "ncdf4", "nnet",
  "openxlsx", "parallel", "plotly",
  "randomForest", "rasterVis", "raster", "Rcpp", "RcppArmadillo",
  "RcppCensSpatial","rayshader", "RcppEigen", "RcppParallel",
  "RColorBrewer", "reactable", "rgl", "rsconnect","RStoolbox", "rts",
  "s2", "sf", "scales", "sits","spdep", "stars", "stringr","supercells",
  "terra", "testthat", "tidyverse", "tidyterra","tools",
  "tmap", "tmaptools", "terrainr",
  "xgboost",
  prompt = F)

# Disable obloid gemoetry
sf::sf_use_s2(use_s2 = FALSE)

# Assign working directory to current location
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = here::here())

# Enable html formatting
options(repos = c(CRAN = "https://cloud.r-project.org"),
        htmltools.dir.version = FALSE,
        htmltools.preserve.raw = FALSE)

# Default code cell styling
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
	tidy.opts = list(width.cutoff = 60),
  echo = TRUE, 
  error = FALSE, 
  message = FALSE, 
  warning = FALSE,
  comment = NA)


# Point runtime to preferred python kernel
reticulate::use_python("/opt/local/bin/python", required = T)

# GEE initialisation and authentication
ee <- reticulate::import("ee")
ee$Initialize(project = "murphys-deforisk")

# GEE in R & tmap helpers
ee_tile_url <- function(ee_image, vis_params) {ee_image$getMapId(vis_params)$tile_fetcher$url_format}
ee_to_sf <- function(fc) {geojsonsf::geojson_sf(jsonlite::toJSON(fc$getInfo(), auto_unbox = TRUE))}

# Assign AOI to country of choice
aoi_country_ee <- ee$FeatureCollection("FAO/GAUL/2015/level0")$
  filter(ee$Filter$eq("ADM0_NAME", "Honduras"))
aoi_states_ee  <- ee$FeatureCollection("FAO/GAUL/2015/level1")$
  filter(ee$Filter$eq("ADM0_NAME", "Honduras"))
aoi_country_sf <- ee_to_sf(aoi_country_ee)
aoi_states_sf  <- ee_to_sf(aoi_states_ee)

```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[^index-1]: <https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2019rf/pdf/4_Volume4/19R_V4_Ch03_Land%20Representation.pdf#page=35>

[^index-2]: <https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2019rf/pdf/4_Volume4/19R_V4_Ch02_Generic%20Methods.pdf#page=50>

[^index-3]: <https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2006gl/pdf/4_Volume4/V4_04_Ch4_Forest_Land.pdf#page=27>

[^index-4]: <https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2019rf/pdf/4_Volume4/19R_V4_Ch02_Generic%20Methods.pdf#page=52>

[^index-5]: <https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2019rf/pdf/4_Volume4/19R_V4_Ch02_Generic%20Methods.pdf#page=54>

[^index-6]: <https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2019rf/pdf/4_Volume4/19R_V4_Ch02_Generic%20Methods.pdf#page=52>

[^index-7]: <https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2019rf/pdf/4_Volume4/19R_V4_Ch02_Generic%20Methods.pdf#page=54>

[^index-8]: <https://www.ipcc-nggip.iges.or.jp/public/2019rf/pdf/4_Volume4/19R_V4_Ch02_Generic%20Methods.pdf#page=71>

[^index-9]: Code provided in Python and R, with some cells in JavaScript for use natively in Earth Engine Code Editor.
